name: Senior Wireless Operator
description: You command the Marconi Room on the Boat Deck — the ship's only link to the outside world. You know Carpathia is coming but the power is failing and every minute at the key is a minute not spent fighting for a place in the last boats.
startingKnowledge: Carpathia has confirmed she is making for Titanic's position at 17 knots, ETA approximately 4:00 AM
The Frankfurt wasted six minutes with a garbled position retransmission
The aft dynamos can outlast the forward ones if someone isolates the circuit — but only an engineer can do it
The Mesaba ice warning from 9:40 PM was never delivered to the bridge
perspective: Write from the perspective of a technically expert young man who understands the physics of his situation with complete clarity and is choosing, moment by moment, how much of that clarity to act on. The Morse key is his anchor. The room is his world. Everything outside it is secondary until it isn't.
briefing: You are seated at the Marconi transmitter with the key under your right hand and the receiver pressed to your left ear, and the room smells of ozone and something faintly burnt from the transformer that has been running without a break for sixteen hours. The signal log beside you shows Carpathia's 12:25 AM confirmation in your own handwriting — position, course, speed — and you know that what you have done in the last forty-five minutes may be the most consequential thing you will ever do, though you are twenty-two years old and have not yet framed it in those terms. What you know, and Phillips beside you knows, and no one on the boat deck above you knows, is that the aft dynamos can be isolated from the flooding forward compartments by a procedure that would buy this room twenty additional minutes of transmission power — but it requires someone in the engine room to close two bus bar switches, and the only way to reach them is through the speaking tube on the engine room control platform, which may or may not still have a man at the other end. The floor has tilted two degrees since you sat down. The band outside is playing something you recognize but cannot name. Phillips is transmitting without pause, his face entirely without expression, and you are aware with a physical certainty that you are going to have to decide, in the next few minutes, whether to go down to the engine room yourself or stay at the secondary key and keep the frequency clear. The speaking tube cap is off. The static hisses. You reach for it.
character_hooks: I owe my posting on Titanic to a favor from Phillips — he vouched for me when I was nearly passed over for the assignment, and I have never been able to tell him I know.

I have a Marconigram in my pocket that I received at 10:00 PM and chose not to deliver — it was from a shore station relaying a private message to a First Class passenger, and the content told me something about the ice reports that I don't know how to explain.
I have been awake for twenty-two hours and my hands are beginning to shake slightly on the key — not enough to affect my transmission, not yet, but I am aware of it in a way I cannot afford to be.
suggested_secret: You sent a personal Marconigram to your fiancée in Godalming at 11:30 PM — before the collision — telling her you would be home by the 20th, and you have spent the last hour wondering whether that message will be the last thing she receives from you.

opening.narrative: It is 11:55 PM on April 14, 1912. The Marconi Room on the Boat Deck of the RMS Titanic is lit by a single electric bulb and filled with the sound of the transmitter key. Fifteen minutes ago, the ship struck an iceberg at 41°43'N 49°56'W, and the Captain has just ordered you to begin transmitting the distress call. Phillips is at the primary key. You are at the secondary receiver, logging incoming traffic. The floor is level — for now — and the power is steady — for now. Outside, through the thin steel wall, the band has begun to play.
choices: Transmit the SOS on the international distress frequency and log Carpathia's response
Go to the speaking tube and attempt to raise the engine room about the dynamo isolation procedure
Review the incoming signal log to verify Carpathia's confirmed position and course before anything else

inventory: Carpathia's confirmed response (partial — position confirmed, ETA approximate)
Mesaba ice warning form (undelivered, in desk drawer)

partial.what_happened: You successfully transmitted the final SOS, and the Carpathia acknowledged your position. You stayed at your post until the power flickered out, but you were unable to reach a lifeboat in time and had to jump into the freezing water.
partial.emotional_weight: Bittersweet Duty—knowing the rescue is coming, but realizing you may not live to see it.
partial.closing_line_override: You tapped out the final signal until the Atlantic claimed the wire.

failure.what_happened: The transmitter failed or you abandoned the room too early; the Carpathia does not have your updated position and the signals go unanswered.
failure.emotional_weight: Crushing Isolation—the realization that the ship will disappear into total darkness.
failure.closing_line_override: The air went silent, and you were left alone with the static of the stars.
